sentences.text: My name is Dorothy Greenstein, nee Kirshenbaum.
K-I-R-S-H-E-N-B-A-U-M.
No, it was Dvorah.
D-V-O-R-A-H.
December 10, 1930 in Otwock, Poland, near Warsaw.
O-T-W-O-C-K.
I am 64 and a half, to be exact.
My mother's name was Golda, and my father's name was Yehoshua, but they called him Shia in Poland.
My father was trained as a cantor, and then as a rabbi, and then as a shochet, a ritual slaughterer.
And then he was also a judge in the community.
In the Jewish community of Otwock.
When somebody had a dispute, and they came to my father.
Whatever he said, it was like God had spoken.
They trusted him very much.
I remember it well, because we had a library.
And I used to like to be in the library, but when they came I was shooed out.
And the two men came in, at first, and then one separately, each.
And then my father used to say three days, he'll look up and see what should be done.
He-- very.
Not just from the Jewish people, but also from the Polish people.
Oh, yes.
I had non-Jewish friends and Jewish friends.
There was a church on our street.
And I had a friend that her older sister played piano.
And that's where I wanted, when I grow up, to be a pianist.
No, because we were seven girls and two brothers.
So my father said, as soon as three girls get married, he'll buy me a piano.
But that was never to be.
The oldest one was Leah, Malka, the next.
After Malka came Rachel.
Tofka, Naomi.
And after the five girls came two boys.
One was Mottel, or Mordecai in Hebrew.
The other one was Yitzchak, or Itzchak we called him in Polish.
Then came two twin sisters, before me, Sarah and Rivka.
But unfortunately, when she was a year old, Rivka died of pneumonia.
And then I was born, shortly afterwards.
So I was, actually, the tenth.
I was the youngest, yes.
Oh, it was a resort area.
We-- our backyard was facing the forest.
The air was excellent.

People used to come to Otwock summer and winter, because summer we had a river from one side, and the trees that gave off-- what do you call those trees with the long, thin-- that give off-- oren, in Hebrew.
In English, I can't think of the name.
I have a tree like that in the front-- similar.
The Christmas trees are made out of that.
Pine trees.
The pine trees were very healthy.
They soak up the moisture.
And our city was known-- people that were coughing or were sick with TB, they used to come.
And in the winter, we had, on the other side, the hills.
They called it the [INAUDIBLE].
And people were coming to ski and to go down with those sleds.
I used to go there often, too, in the winter.
Not on Saturday.
Yes, we were Orthodox.
But my father allowed us to play with non-Jewish friends, because he knew that whatever we learn at home, that's what's going to stay with us.
And that's how it was.
And thanks to this, that my father was so, in a way, liberal, we girls could survive among the Polish people.
Because we spoke Polish very well.
The boys went to cheder, to a special school.
But the girls-- my father didn't like the Jewish private schools, because they didn't teach Polish enough.
So we took-- he took a Hebrew teacher and he came home to the girls.
And of course, the religion, we got at home-- the holidays.
And that-- we had another teacher that taught us the prayers.
So we had two, actually, the girls, two teachers.
But I forgot it all during the war when I was on Polish papers as a Polish girl.
I-- it just flew out my head.
I guess I just blocked it out.
Oh, yes.
I guess through the papers, we knew that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were singing his song.
Can I sing it Polish?
I hope I remember it.
[SINGING IN POLISH]
All the Polish people were singing it.
They were sure that the Germans cannot penetrate into Poland.
He was the marshal.
That means the head of the army.
And he is a big hero, and nobody will dare take anything from us, which means Poland, because with us is Smigly-Ridz.
Yes.
When the war broke out, it was just September, so when the Germans came in a few days later, they did not allow any Jewish kids to go to school.
So actually, I only finished two grades in Polish school.
But when things got a little bit more stabilized and we went to the ghetto, my sister, Tofka, she was the one that went to university before the war, so she got another girl my age.
And the other girl's parents paid, and we were studying.
So we made up about two grades in the ghetto.
Tofka must be 81, now.
So I'm 64 and a half.
She was about 16, 17 years older.
Yeah.
Oh, yes.
When they marched into our city.
And we all went out to the street to see them.
And I remember we had a neighbor that-- a friend of my father's.
He was like Hertzl, tall and broad.
And-- but he had a white beard, and he greeted them.
And he answered like this to them.
Oh, yes, because they were awesome-- just terrible.
The way they marched, and they were spotless and shoes shining and boots clicking and marching and singing.
And the armored cars and everything, the motorcycles-- it was just a terrible sight.
Well, in a way, I was secure being with everybody.
So as long as I was in the home, I wasn't that frightened.
But later on, when they came to arrest my father-- that was the same year-- we had a very long house, because my father built it to his specifications.
And we happened to be in the kitchen, and the entrance was through a gate-- through a narrow gate to the back of the house.
And we saw soldiers coming, Germans.
So we just had a feeling they came for my father.
So my father was urged to run away to a neighbor, across the street.
And sure enough, they came in.
And they said, we just want to see the rabbi.
We're not going to do anything to him.
We just want to see him, and we want to see how a rabbi looks.
Because he was very known, and that's what the Germans did-- they arrested, right away, the very prominent people.
And we said, we don't-- my sister and my mother-- we don't know where they are.
So they arrested my two brothers, and they took them to jail until the-- we can find the rabbi, then they're going to stay in jail.
And we had a lot of trouble, because my one brother was very orthodox, and he wouldn't even drink water.
The one that isn't alive.
The other one adapted well, and he was eating a little and drinking.
But this one wouldn't even drink water from a dish.
Mottel, Mordecai.
And he was ordained as a rabbi when he was 17.
He was very brilliant.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So we tried to find a way, how we can get him out.
And it was really a big tragedy that time, because we knew he's going to die.
He was never strong anyways.
My other brother played football.
He-- he would study with the rabbi, and once the rabbi went through the Bible and the explanations, out he went through the window.
Why was that?
Because he was very bright, too, and he didn't want to listen to it again.
My father didn't know he did that, because he always knew his part on Friday nights when he asked him.
But a few months later, they found out.
The teacher told my father, so he really got a big licking.
I remember my father was sick for weeks after that, that he had to--
Discipline him, that he shouldn't run away.
He should stay there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father, that time when he went across the street, my older sister took him to Warsaw.
They dressed him like a woman.
And he had big, blue eyes, so they put on a kerchief that only the eyes were showing.
And he wasn't supposed to talk on the train.
The oldest, [INAUDIBLE], Leah.
Because she was in Warsaw at that time.
She went to Warsaw before the ghetto was even done.
She was married and had a baby.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So he stayed there for many months until the whole city was sorry for my brother, because he would have died.
He can't drink.
He can't eat.
Can't give him any food.
So they collected money.
And my sister, Tofka, she knew German because she studied languages.
And they collected money, and she took a suitcase of money.
And she went to [INAUDIBLE].
He was like the mayor of the city, the overseer.
And she told German to him.
She was a good-looking girl.
She wore those corsets, and she tied herself in.
Many times I put my knee there and yanked it so she would have a nice figure.
And he was very impressed with her German.
And then she opened the suitcase, and he saw the money.
It turned his head.
There was nobody else in the office, so he figured he'll have this money for himself.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So he gave-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what should I say?
A permission for the brothers to be released.
And he took the money, of course.
And that was it.
Now, he was Polish or German?
German.
He was a German overseer of the city.
A mayor, whatever you want to call him.
But he was the head of the city.
If there was any problem, you would go to him.
He was a very cruel man, because I remember he rode on a horse.

And whenever he saw a Jew with a beard-- because Polish people those years did not wear beards-- and-- or the payos, the-- the side locks, that some had tzitzit showing out, so he knew for sure this is a Jew-- he would just hit them from the horse with his whip.
This I experienced, and I've seen it myself.
Everybody was scared of [INAUDIBLE].
But my sister figured she has no choice.
We have to rescue our brother, because he would have died for sure.
You see, the other brother was playing football and he was outside a lot, where my-- Mottel, Mordecai, he was always studying.
So he was always pale, and he didn't eat too much because his head was full of-- he studied the Kabbalah, against my father's wishes, even.

When my father wasn't there, he'd go to the library to-- in our house-- and take the books and study so he wouldn't Now, my other brother, Yitzchak, that is in Canada, he just called me and he's some philosopher.
He tells me things about the Kabbalah that he studies on his own, too.
Yeah, he has a beautiful library in his house in Canada, as well.
Wherever-- whenever there is a new book out, whether it's English, or Yiddish, or Hebrew, he always buys it.
They came back home, and somehow-- it took another few months.
And we went into the ghetto, so my father came back.
Unfortunately, yeah.
We had an old father living with us that came from Warsaw.
He couldn't walk, and he was with us.
In the library, he was living.
My grand-- my father's father.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We just had a room and a kitchen.
But of course, there were-- one sister was already in Israel from 1935.
That's Malka.
And Leah was married.
They were married the same year, and the same month, and the same day-- one in Israel, and one at home.
I remember my sister's wedding.
It was September.
I was almost-- was it four?
I think I was almost four. 1934, '35.
I was four or five years old.
I remember the wedding very, very well, because so much changes happened.
They took the furniture out.
And there was a store off the kitchen, a door that the credenza was like this one but in white, so they took it away.
The food should be in there, because there were no refrigerators, those days.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I remember it vividly, the wedding of my sister.
Well, we had the-- here we had four, five rooms.
There, we had a room and a kitchen.
So you can imagine what we took with us-- very little.
The books, of course, they were the first thing my father took.
But as far as the furniture and the dining room, we just couldn't take that.
So I know the clock was sold for food, before we moved yet.
We had a grandfather's clock.
It's funny that to this day I'm looking for one that is-- looks like it.
And if I could find it, I wouldn't care how much it is.
I would buy it.
But I haven't seen one yet.
The grandfather was then very old and not well.
And we used to-- there were a few short weeks that we had this transition to get into.
So we had the house, but we moved to the-- to the ghetto.
And the father, my grandfather, was left behind.
And a neighbor gave him food, and we came to give him food, too.
But when they really closed off, tightly, the ghetto, just a few days before, my grandfather died.
And were you able to--
I remember that, too.
In a way, it was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a relief for my father, because we couldn't take him with us.
There was nowhere to put him.
I mean, we were so many kids.
And three sisters were married.
Tofka was married, too.
She was married before we moved in 1940.
Before we moved to the ghetto, she was married.
So with the three girls married, and we were, what, six?
Six kids in one small room with a kitchen.
It was very, very difficult.
It almost was one on top of the other.
Were you able to give your grandfather a proper burial?
Yes, we did.
We did.
In the ghetto, I remember my mother had a father, Joseph, Yosef, that he died in the ghetto.
And I remember very well when they took him up to the synagogue.
And my father had a what-- eulogy for him?
And I remember how he said, it's now an honor to die, because you know where you're buried.
And the future is very unsure.
So therefore, to die now and be buried properly is like an honor, a big thing, a [HEBREW].
So that was another sad thing, that the two grandfathers died.
Well, my grandmother lived in Warsaw.
And I used to go with my father-- I don't know how often, once a month-- to Warsaw to visit them.
Sometimes I went, and sometimes I didn't.
So I don't remember her funeral.
But my other grandmother's funeral, I remember.
I was a little girl, maybe five years old.
They took me to the cemetery, and I remember how she was buried in the white shrouds.
And she was lowered to the grave.
I was shaken up, that time, but somehow they took me with.
The one thing I remember-- they didn't take me, and I was crying terribly-- when my sister went to Israel.
She had to go, first, to Warsaw.
Everybody went to the train.
I stayed with a babysitter, and I screamed.
I wanted to go, too.
So I was four years old in a few months, so I remember all these things very vividly.
There wasn't enough food, and people were going around hungry.
In a way, we were luckier than most of all, because my sister had a store.
And she left all merchandise when she went to Warsaw, so we were selling the stuff.
Like, materials and curtain stuff and stockings and things like that for women.
So we were selling.
And my sister, Rachel, she really looked like a Polish girl with the nose and the blue eyes.
So she would go out of the ghetto.
You see, our city had three ghettos.
It was a big city, and somehow they made three ghettos from it.
But at night, for curfew, they closed it.
And one couldn't visit the other one, because there was a space between each one.
So she was going to the Polish side.
And she was buying stuff, selling and buying stuff.
And I remember, myself, I used to climb through a wall-- maybe it was six feet or more, such a block wall-- to go to the Polish side and buy salt, sugar, potatoes, and some bread.
So I would go through a special site.
You see, the thing is that in the ghetto you did not need Germans to watch it.
You had a post, an outpost, where trucks or wagons came in.
And the rest was not guarded, because the Polish people were very good guard.
When they saw a Jew, they made sure that they turned them in.
But I was a kid with long braids.
And we lived in another side of the city, so the people did not know me very well.
See, if I would be in the middle ghetto, they would know me, because that's where we lived.
But we were put in the other side.
Because you couldn't go to which ghetto you wanted.
They just assigned you.
You go to this side, and you go here, and you go there.
They just tried to uproot you.
You shouldn't be among people that you know.
But sometimes it worked out for the best, because if I climbed through this wall, nobody knew me on the other side.
So I went to the stores, and I bought what I had to.
And then I would throw it over, and then climb the fence.
No, no.
I had money.
My older sister did that.
From selling the stuff.
She would sell it, and then she would get money.
Not much.
He couldn't do much.
He prayed a lot.
I remember when we learned that the ghetto's going to be-- a-- what-- what should I say?
Shipped out to someplace?
They called it replacement, resettlement.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We didn't know the full extent of what it's going to be, because other people were replaced to our city.
And then they replaced this city.
But unfortunately, our city was sent to Treblinka.
It must've been in the summer, because I remember it wasn't the new year-- the Jewish new year-- and all the Jews went and put on the white coats, and the tallis.
And they went to pray with the shofar-- as if it was new year's-- to save them.
This, I remember.
And then my father was thinking.
He said, well, one is in Israel, and one is in Warsaw with her husband.
The other one was in the camp Karczew.
Tofka.
And my brother, also, was caught to the camp.
They just came to the ghetto, and they caught people.
Or they had a list.
I know my brother-in-law was no physical laborer, because he was short and he wasn't really too well.
But they took him to be the administrator of the camp, and my sister went with him.
But when they caught my brother, they took him to the same camp in Karczew.
That was Yitzchak.
And the other one, too.
They caught both of them.
Yitzchak was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was more flexible, so he adjusted.
But Mottel, my other brother, the rabbi, he wasn't very flexible.
And he wouldn't-- the same thing-- he wouldn't eat what everybody did.
He had to have his own dishes and cook his own meals, to be sure that it's Kosher.
Even in the labor camp.
And when they send them out to build roads, because that's what this camp was for, to build roads for the tanks-- not for the tanks, for the motorcycle and trucks.
Because they didn't have paved roads in-- around Karczew.
It was all muddy when it was raining, so they used to get stuck.
And that's what this crew was for.
It was a few hundred people that they were sent in different directions to build the road.
So when my brother went on the road, he looked at his watch.
It was time to pray, so he stopped.
And here everybody yelled at him.
You're going to get killed.
You can't do that.
And he just wouldn't listen.
So it was unfortunate that the day before the liquidation of the camp, he came home from--
--the camp.
Karczew.
Of Karczew.
That was-- oh, the city.
You're right, the city.
The camp stayed there longer.
And he came home because he couldn't take it from other people criticizing him that he's cooking for himself and he's praying at the proper times.
And then we knew that the city's going to be shipped out.
So my father gathered the girls.
We were Sarah, Naomi, Rachel, and me--
